coredata.dc:title: Green light-emitting polyepinephrine-based fluorescent organic dots and its application in intracellular metal ions sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Zhong Feng
Li, Ting Ting
Xu, Xiao Lei
Liu, Yi Yao
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we present a class of bio-dots, polyepinephrine (PEP)-based fluorescent organic dots (PEP-FODs) for selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+. The PEP-FODs were derived from epinephrine via self-polymerization at relatively low temperature down to 60°C with low cytotoxicity and relative long lifetime (7.24ns). The surface morphology and optical properties of the synthesized PEP-FODs were characterized. We found that the diameters of PEP-FODs were mainly distributed in the narrow range of 2–4nm with an average diameter of 2.9nm. An optimal emission peak located at 490nm was observed when the green light-emitting PEP-FODs were excited at 400nm. It is discovered that Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+can strongly quench the fluorescence of PEP-FODs through the nonradiative electron-transfer. The detection limit of 0.16, 0.67, and 0.15μM was obtained for Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+, respectively. The independent sensing platform of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+could be established by using NaF as a complexing agent and by regulating the reaction time between NaF and metal ions. Cell viability studies reveal that the as-prepared PEP-FODs possess good solubility and biocompatibility, making it as excellent imaging nanoprobes for intracellular Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+sensing. The developed PEP-FODs might hold great promise to broaden applications in nanotechnology and bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyepinephrine
Fluorescent organic dots
Biosensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of PEP-FODs toward metal ions
Possible mechanism of the PEP-FODs metal ion-mediated fluorescence quenching

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( Nos. 21273174, 31470969 and 81471785 ), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (Nos. CSTC-2013jjB00002 and CSTC-2015jcyjB50001 ), and the Innovation Foundation of Chongqing City for Postgraduate ( CYB14052 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis process of green emitting polyepinephrine fluorescent organic dots from epinephrine. The digital images show the color change before and after synthesis of PEP-FODs.
UV−vis absorption spectra of EP and PEP-FODs and fluorescence spectra of PEP-FODs. Insets are the photographs of PEP-FODs solution under visible light and UV light.

(A) Fluorescence intensity changes of PEP-FODs in the presence of different metal ions (1mM) listed from left to right: Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Li+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, K+, and Na+. (B) Fluorescence intensity changes of PEP-FODs in the presence of 1mM Fe3+and 0.5M NaF. (C) Fluorescence intensity changes of PEP-FODs in the presence of 1mM Fe2+and 0.5M NaF with 1min and 90min reaction, respectively. (B) Fluorescence intensity changes of PEP-FODs in the presence of 1mM Cu2+and 0.5M NaF.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PEP-FODs (250μgmL−1) with different concentrations of Fe2+(from a to n: 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 1200, and 1500 μM, respectively). (B) The curve of the fluorescence quenching efficiency versus Fe2+concentration. The inset in (B) is the linear calibration plot for Fe2+detection. (C) Fluorescence spectra of PEP-FODs (250μgmL−1) with different concentrations of Fe3+(from a to l: 2, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 200, 300, 500, 1000, 1200, and 1500 μM, respectively). (D) The curve of the fluorescence quenching efficiency versus Fe3+concentration. The inset in (D) is the linear calibration plot for Fe3+detection. (E) Fluorescence spectra of PEP-FODs (250μgmL−1) with different concentrations of Cu2+(from a to m: 0.5, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 1200, and 1500 μM, respectively). (F) The curve of the fluorescence quenching efficiency versus Cu 2+concentration. The inset in (F) is the linear calibration plot for Cu 2+detection.
(A) Fluorescence mechanism of PEP-FODs and (B) the electron transfer process from PEP-FODs to metal ions.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of HeLa cells after 6h incubation with 250μgmL−1 PEP-FODs, and then incubated with 1mM Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+for 1h, respectively. HeLa cells incubated with 250μgmL−1 PEP-FODs and then incubated with equal volume cell culture medium instead of metal ions was used as the control group. Scale bar is 10µm.

head.ce:title: Green light-emitting polyepinephrine-based fluorescent organic dots and its application in intracellular metal ions sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biophysics, School of Life Science and Technology, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu, Sichuan 610054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biophysics, School of Life Science and Technology, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we present a class of bio-dots, polyepinephrine (PEP)-based fluorescent organic dots (PEP-FODs) for selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+. The PEP-FODs were derived from epinephrine via self-polymerization at relatively low temperature down to 60°C with low cytotoxicity and relative long lifetime (7.24ns). The surface morphology and optical properties of the synthesized PEP-FODs were characterized. We found that the diameters of PEP-FODs were mainly distributed in the narrow range of 2–4nm with an average diameter of 2.9nm. An optimal emission peak located at 490nm was observed when the green light-emitting PEP-FODs were excited at 400nm. It is discovered that Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+can strongly quench the fluorescence of PEP-FODs through the nonradiative electron-transfer. The detection limit of 0.16, 0.67, and 0.15μM was obtained for Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+, respectively. The independent sensing platform of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+could be established by using NaF as a complexing agent and by regulating the reaction time between NaF and metal ions. Cell viability studies reveal that the as-prepared PEP-FODs possess good solubility and biocompatibility, making it as excellent imaging nanoprobes for intracellular Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+sensing. The developed PEP-FODs might hold great promise to broaden applications in nanotechnology and bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Advances of fluorescent nanoparticles have created new avenues for drug delivery (Lucky et al., 2015), bioimaging (Guo et al., 2014), bioanalysis (Liang et al., 2014), and other biomedical applications (Weissleder et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2014c), owing to the remarkable optical properties. Various fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles (FINs), such as fluorescent carbon dots (Hu et al., 2015), fluorescent metal nanoclusters (Chen et al., 2015), semiconductor quantum dots (Giansante et al., 2015), silicon quantum dots (Lin and Zhang , 2012; Lin et al., 2012), and Ln (Eu or Tb) ion doped nanomaterials (Hui et al., 2012) have been reported and extensively investigated for biomedical applications. Although FINs possess incomparable fluorescent properties and have been widely employed for bioimaging and bioanalysis, most FINs have many inherent drawbacks, such as non-biodegradable, biological toxicity, and relatively difficult synthesis (Choi et al., 2007; Kairdolf et al., 2013). Thus, the searching of alternative nanoprobes with high fluorescent efficiency, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity is of great research interest.

Fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) have been regarded as the promising candidates for bioimaging and diagnostic applications because of their good biocompatibility, biodegradability, low toxicity, and adjustable functionalities (An et al., 2002; Kaeser et al., 2013). A variety of FONs based on conjugate polymers (Nie et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2013), small organic molecules (Yu et al., 2011; Yu et al., 2009), and aggregation induced emission materials (Zhang et al., 2014b; Zhang et al., 2015) have been synthesized and reported for biomedical applications over the past few decades. One of the most applicable strategies for fabrication of FONs is the controlled self-assembly of monodispersed-conjugated chromophores or oligomers (Cui et al., 2011; Wu et al., 2015). However, this self-assembled structure is unstable in water, and the decrease of fluorescence quantum yield is often observed due to the strong hydrophobic interactions of FONs in water (Xie et al., 2001; Zhang et al., 2012). These disadvantages severely limit their bioanalysis and biomedical applications.

To overcome some of these defects, fluorescent organic nanoparticles derived from self-polymerization of organic small molecules have been reported, such as fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles, cytosine bio-dots, etc (Guo et al., 2013; Yildirim and Bayindir, 2014; Zhang et al., 2012). For instance, Tseng and coworkers (Lin et al., 2015) reported an inspiring article on the synthesis and application of polydopamine organic nanodots for metal ions detection. The polydopamine organic nanodots have high specificity towards Fe3+. And they further described the fluorescence quenching mechanism through the coordination of catachols group on the polydopamine and the ferric ions. Although remarkable biocompatibility and high quantum yields of polydopamine nanoparticles have been demonstrated (Zhang et al., 2012; Yildirim and Bayindir, 2014), some drawbacks, such as nonuniform morphologies and uncontrolled dimensions at nanoscale level, will produce unwanted interference for multiplexed intracellar imaging and biosensing (Xiong et al., 2014). In this contribution, we propose a polydopamine analogues based bio-dots, polyepinephrine (PEP)-based fluorescent organic dots (PEP-FODs), which were synthesized from epinephrine (EP) via self-polymerization at relatively low temperature with a uniform diameter. The PEP-FODs were completely water-soluble and remarkably stable under extreme pH conditions and high ionic strengths. Hence, a label free and rapid sensing platform for metal ions detection, such as Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+, was developed based on the nonradiative electron-transfer induced fluorescence quenching of PEP-FODs. It was discovered that the nanoprobe showed low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, and was successfully utilized for intracellular imaging of multiple metal ions. To our knowledge, polyepinephrine-based fluorescent organic dots have yet to be investigated and this is the first report on the synthesis and sensing applications of PEP-FODs.

Epinephrine was obtained from Xiya Reagent Co., Ltd., (Chengdu, China). 3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)−2-(4-sulfophenyl)−2H-tetrazolium inner salt (MTS) was purchased from Promega (Madison, WI). CuSO4, FeSO4, FeCl3, and other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Aqueous solutions used in this work were prepared with ultrapure water (>18MΩcm). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image was recorded on a FEI Tecnai G20 microscope (FEI Company, USA) operated at 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source. The size of PEP-FODs was measured using dynamic light scattering with a zetasizer (Malvern zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, UK). A time-resolved fluorescence spectrum and absolute quantum yield were obtained from a FLSP920 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh instruments Ltd., UK). UV–vis absorption spectra were conducted on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a 1cm quartz cell in the emission mode. The slit width was 10 and 10nm for excitation and emission, respectively.

Epinephrine (100mg) was dissolved in mixture of 100mL of 20mM NaOH solution and 40mL of isopropyl alcohol under vigorous stirring. After overnight stirring under reflux at 60°C, PEP-FODs were obtained. The resulting solution was filtered with micropore filter (0.22µm) to remove aggregates formed during preparation. After removal of the solvent by rotary evaporation, the residue was re-dispersed in water.

In a typical run, a certain concentration of metal ions was added to 250μgmL−1 PEP-FODs solution, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature. For the independent detection of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+: The NaF solution (0.5M) was first mixed with 1mM Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+for a certain time, respectively. Then, the mixture was added to the PEP-FODs solution (250μgmL−1) and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

HeLa (a human cervical carcinoma cell line) cells were obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). Briefly, 1×104 cells/well were seeded in 96-well plate at 37°C. After 24h of cell attachment, the cells were incubated with 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, and 250µgmL–1 PDA-FODs for 6h and 12h, respectively. Then the PEP-FODs were removed and cells were gently washed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) for three times. The cell viability was evaluated using the MTS assay. The CellTiter 96® AQ ueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay kit was used following the manufacturer’s instruction. MTS (10μL) was added to each well, and then incubated for an additional 1h at 37°C. The absorbance was measured at 490nm by a microplate reader (ELx808™ Bio Tek Instruments, Inc. USA). The cell viability was defined as the absorbance ratio in the presence of PEP-FODs to that untreated controls (A sample/A control).

The HeLa cells (1×104 cells/well) were seeded in a 24-well plate and cultured in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 cell culture medium supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS), 2mM glutamine, 100UmL–1 penicillin, and 100UmL–1streptomycin at 37°C in a humidified condition of 5% CO2 for 12h. The PEP-FODs (250 μg mL−1) were added to the cell culture, and the HeLa cells were incubated for another 6h at 37°C. Then, HeLa cells were washed three times with the medium to remove the remaining PEP-FODs. Experiments to assess Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+uptake were performed for another 1h in the same medium supplemented with 1mM Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+, respectively. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with fresh PBS solution (10mM, pH 7.4), the fluorescence images were acquired with a confocal laser scanning microscope (model TCS SP, Leica, Wetzlar, Germany) with the excitation wavelength of 405nm.

As shown in Fig. 1, EP was first self-polymerized in alkaline solution to form PEP. The occurrence of reaction can be observed by the color change from colorless to brown. Due to the similar structure to dopamine, we assumed that the possible working mechanism for self-polymerization of EP might be the oxidation of catechol in an alkaline environment (Dreyer et al., 2012). Effect of synthesis temperature on fluorescence behavior of PEP-FODs was investigated with the result given in Fig. S1, discovering that a temperature at 60°C can produce well fluorescence intensity. Treatment time is also found to have influence on the fluorescence intensity, which increases with the time up to 120min and then keeps almost constant (Fig. S2).

The morphology of PEP-FODs was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). As displayed in Fig. S3A, the PEP-FODs were uniform in size and well monodispersed. We found that the diameters of PEP-FODs were mainly distributed in the narrow range of 2–4nm with an average diameter of 2.9nm by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. S3B). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were employed to identify the surface functional groups on the PEP-FODs (Fig. S4). The characteristic spectral peaks of several functional groups were observed, such as –OH, N–H, C–O, and C–N.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed to investigate the composition of the PEP-FODs. The XPS survey spectrum of the PEP-FODs shows the presence of C 1s (285.44eV), N 1s (399.9eV), and O 1s (531.27eV) with atomic percentages of 54.88%, 5.55%, 39.57%, respectively (Fig. S5A). A high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s indicated the presence of C-C (284.83eV), C-N (286.3eV), C-O (288.02eV), and C˭O (289.25eV) bonds (Fig. S5B). This revealed that the PEP-FODs were rich in hydrophilic groups, which was consistent with the corresponding FTIR spectrum. The O 1s peak at 531.19eV shown in Fig. S5C confirmed the presence of C˭O. The peak at 535.63eV in O 1s spectrum has been tentatively ascribed to chemisorbed O2 and/or water (Fig. S5C) (Plomp et al., 2009). One fitted peak at 399.89eV in N 1s spectrum was assigned to N-(C)3, implying that the EP might be successfully polymerized into PEP (Fig. S5D).

The optical properties of the PEP-FODs were investigated by the UV−vis absorption and the fluorescence spectra. The UV−vis absorption spectrum of the PEP-FODs shows a higher and broader band absorption compared with that of EP, which is ascribed to the n−π* transition of C=O. The photoluminescence and photoluminescence excitation spectra of the PEP-FODs solution were recorded ( Fig. 2). An optimal emission peak located at 490nm was observed when the PEP-FODs were excited at 400nm by a Xe lamp from the fluorescence spectrometer with a Stokes shift of 90nm. Insets of Fig. 3 are the photographs of PEP-FODs solution, which were taken under visible and UV lights. The light brown PEP-FODs aqueous solution emitted strong green fluorescence upon being irradiated by a 365nm lamp. As displayed in Fig. S6, the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior was observed. When the excitation wavelength changed from 400 to 480nm, the emission peak shifted from 490 to 560nm and the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly. The absolute quantum yield (QY) of PEP-FODs was measured to be 18% (Fig. S7), which was higher than those of some reported carbon dots and graphene quantum dots (Dong et al., 2012a; Li et al., 2014a; Lin and Zhang, 2012).

The photostability of PEP-FODs toward ambient environments, such as extreme pH or high ionic strengths, plays a vital role in practical sensing applications. Fig. S8 presented the fluorescence intensities of PEP-FODs at different pH values. It can be seen be that the PEP-FODs had strong fluorescence activities in the pH range of 3–11, indicating the high stability under extreme pH conditions. Then, we investigated the effect of ionic strength on the stability of PEP-FODs in different concentrations of NaCl. It can be seen from Fig. S9 that the fluorescence intensity of PEP-FODs remained constant with increasing ionic strength from 0 to 1M. The remarkable photostability could be understood in terms of the unique chemical structure that no highly ionized functional groups located on the surfaces of PEP-FODs. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of PEP-FODs decreased slightly even after 6 months (Fig. S10), indicating excellent photostability. Taking the results above, we may conclude that the PEP-FODs possess high photostability even under extreme conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of PEP-FODs toward metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The PEP-FODs might be potentially used for sensing metal ions owing to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups. We explored the feasibility of using such PEP-FODs for the detection of metal ions. Fig. 3(A) shows the quenching efficiency of PEP-FODs after adding various metal ions. Significantly fluorescence quenching was observed after the addition of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+in aqueous solution, respectively. And other ions showed a relatively weak fluorescence quenching. It is very encouraging that the present PEP-FODs indicate the feasibility for Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+determination and the great potential use in real practical application.

The response rates of the fluorescence signal of the PEP-FODs to Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+were then investigated. It can be seen from Fig. S11 that the fluorescence intensities of the PEP-FODs were significantly quenched within 1min as soon as 1mM Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+were added to the PEP-FODs solution and then remained stable. This result illustrates that the fluorescence quenching of the PEP-FODs by Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+is rapid, indicating a promising application in a fast sensing of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+without strict time control.

Due to the similar fluorescence quenching ability among Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+, it is a great challenge to carry out the independent analysis of the ions with high specificity. Fortunately, this issue can be circumvented by using NaF as a complexing agent for Fe3+(Fig. 3(B)). Due to the chelation of Fe3+with NaF, a low fluorescence intensity change was observed. For Fe2+sensing, the fluorescence was quenched with the addition of fresh Fe2+solution (Fig. 3(C)). However, the fluorescence intensity stayed in a high level after 90min incubation of Fe2+and NaF. This might be the reason that Fe2+could be oxidized to Fe3+in the presence of O2 and then chelated with NaF, leading to the strong fluorescence. Interestingly, the addition of Cu2+to PEP-FODs solution in the presence of NaF gave no effect on the detection of Cu2+(Fig. 3(D)). The chelation time effect of Fe3+, Fe2+, and Cu2+with NaF on fluorescence intensities is displayed in Fig. S12. The control experiment revealed that the fluorescence intensity of PEP-FODs barely changed in the presence of NaF (Fig. S13). Thus, specific determination of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+was achieved by using NaF as a complexing agent and regulating the reaction time.

On the basis of the discussion above, the sensitivities of the sensor for the detection of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+have been investigated. With the addition of various concentrations of Fe2+in the range of 0.5−1500μM to the PEP-FODs solution, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased ( Fig. 4(A)). The quenching efficiency ((F 0−F)/F 0) showed a good linear relationship versus the Fe2+concentration ranged from 0.5 to 50μM (Fig. 4(B)), where F 0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Fe2+, respectively. The linear regression equation is (F 0−F)/F 0=0.0071 C+0.0341 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9909. Fig. 4(C) exhibits the fluorescence spectra of PEP-FODs solution with the addition of Fe3+. And Fig. 4(D) shows that the quenching efficiency has a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ranged from 2 to 200μM. The linear regression equation for Fe3+detection is (F 0-F)/F 0=0.0031 C+0.0034 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9946, where F 0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. When Cu2+with concentration ranged from 0.5 to 1500μM was added to the PEP-FODs solution, respectively, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased (Fig. 4(E)). Fig. 4(F) presents the good linear relationship of the quenching efficiency with the concentrations of Cu2+in the range of 0.5–100μM, and the linear regression equation is (F 0-F)/F 0=0.0051 C+0.0383 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9895, where F 0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. The detection limit of 0.16, 0.67, and 0.15μM was obtained based on 3σ/slope (where σ is the standard deviation) for Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+, respectively. It worth noting that, the spectra slightly changed with an observation of shoulder peak around 460nm with the addition of metal ions, which might due to the changes of microenvironment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the PEP-FODs metal ion-mediated fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para:
Enlightened by some reported studies, the working mechanism for the metal ion-mediated PEP-FODs fluorescence quenching might be the nonradiative electron-transfer (Li et al., 2014a; Zhang et al., 2014a). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed to investigate the electrochemical properties of the PEP-FODs, and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels were calculated from the onset oxidation (E ox) and reduction (E red) potentials according to the empirical formula (Cho et al., 2010; Liang et al., 2006):
(1)
E
HOMO
= - e
(
E
ox
+ 4.4
)
(2)
E
LUMO
= - e
(
E
red
+ 4.4
)

Fig. S14 displays the cyclic voltammograms of PEP-FODs on a glassy carbon electrode in 0.1M Bu4NPF6/acetonitrile solution. The E red is determined to be −0.49V. The corresponding E LUMO is calculated to be −3.91eV. The HOMO value could be calculated according to E HOMO=E LUMO−E g (Fan et al., 2014), where E g is the energy band gap. E g was estimated to be 3.4eV, which was obtained from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum (Fig. S15). Thus, the HOMO value is calculated to be −7.31eV. In the absence of metal ions, the PEP-FODs exhibited high fluorescence with an emission energy of 2.53eV ( Fig. 5(A)). Previous studies reported that phenolic hydroxyl groups exhibited good binding affinity for ferric ions (Qu et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014a), and amine groups can coordinate selectively with copper ions. (Dong et al., 2012b; Li et al., 2014b; Zong et al., 2011). Thus, when ferric ions and copper ions were added, the combination of ferric ions with phenolic hydroxyl and copper ions with amine groups occurred, leading to the splitting of d orbital of ferric ions and copper ions. Due to the reducing nature of ferrous iron and generation of hydrated electrons and/or radicals in its photooxidation, the mechanism of Fe2+-induced fluorescence quenching is not unequivocal. According to some previous studies (Hou et al., 2013; Ondigo et al., 2013), Fe2+might coordinate with nitrogen atoms and oxygen atoms to form Fe2+-PEP complexes. Therefore, the electrons in the excited state of PEP-FODs will transfer to the 3d orbits of Fe3+and Cu2+(Fig. 5(B)), facilitating nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation and resulting in significant fluorescence quenching (Li et al., 2014a; Mei et al., 2012).

To further verify our proposed quenching mechanism, we acquired time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the PEP-FODs before and after addition of metal ions (Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+), respectively (Fig. S16). It can be seen that the average fluorescence lifetime of PEP-FODs was 7.24ns after fitting, indicating a fast exciton recombination process. The lifetime of PEP-FODs decreased to 3.64, 5.41, and 6.15ns in the presence of Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+, respectively. The significantly reduced fluorescence lifetime revealed the occurrence of dynamics quenching and further confirmed that there was a fast electron transfer process in the PEP-FODs based sensing system (Fan et al., 2009).

To demonstrate its biological applications, MTS assays were conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the PEP-FODs to HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. S17, the cell viability was estimated to be greater than 80% with 5–250 μg mL−1 of PEP-FODs for 6 and 12h, which indicated the remarkable biocompatibility. When the concentration of PEP-FODs is higher than 100μgmL–1, the cellular viabilities decreased after 6h cell culture. This might be due to the adaptive response to cellular stress and cells activate a death program (apoptosis) when tolerance limits of PEP-FODs are exceeded (Kultz, 2005; Tang et al., 2015). After the cell adaptation process, the normal cell began to growth and proliferation (Magrez et al., 2006), resulting in a higher cellular viability of 12h.

We then evaluated the intracellular distribution of PEP-FODs. As shown in Fig. 6, cells treated with PEP-FODs displayed a strong green fluorescence in cytoplasm. For the control, no fluorescence was observed in cytoplasm. The excellent cell uptake and intracellular imaging of PEP-FODs endow a great potential for monitoring metal ions in living cells. Hence, experiments were conducted to further demonstrate the feasibility of the prepared PEP-FODs for imaging intracellular Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+in HeLa cells. It can be seen from Fig. 6, when exogenous Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+were introduced into the PEP-FODs pretreated HeLa cells, as expected, very weak intracellular green fluorescence was observed. All these results revealed that the PEP-FODs were low toxic, biocompatible, and could serve as an effective intracellular Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+imaging nanoprobe.

In summary, a new class of bio-dots, green emitting PEP-based fluorescent organic dots, has been developed via a straightforward one-pot oxidation method. The water soluble PEP-FODs are remarkably stable against extreme pH and ionic strengths. Moreover, we have demonstrated that the PEP-FODs are low toxic and membrane permeable. The excellent fluorescence properties can be utilized for label free Fe2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+sensing, even in living cell. Given the facile synthetic process, remarkable water solubility and biocompatibility of the PEP-FODs, such type of fluorescent organic dots might be promising for further biomedical applications in future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21273174, 31470969 and 81471785), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (Nos. CSTC-2013jjB00002 and CSTC-2015jcyjB50001), and the Innovation Foundation of Chongqing City for Postgraduate (CYB14052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.bios.2016.04.041.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
